sentences.text: I was-- received a card from my mother.
And she wrote me on that card the place when-- where-- on our parents lived, that she goes there.
So I know that she is going to go there.
I didn't know about what happened with my father at that point.
My brother was in Switzerland.
So we-- we so we go back now to the-- we arriving in Austria-- and-- or Germany-- that time.
And we go to a place called Fehring, F-E-H-R-I-N-G.
And that was the first place when we are inside.
And I would like to make an issue here.
Because that's, to me, very important too that we were supposed to receive a bread for four of us.
So then we had to be in line.
And-- and then I received the bread when I was only the third.
And eventually, I became aware of what was really happening.
And so even in the worst circumstances, you will see that some people are trying to be helpful.
So they didn't make a mistake.
They made a mistake intentionally.
Right.
And then I went-- then we went from there on to a place called Kloch, K-L-O-C-H.
And we were accommodated in a school.
And about two weeks after we were exposed to these things in lie-- with-- in Sopron, I started to have a very high temperature, and I got very sick.
I went to see the doctor.
Of course, the doctor didn't have anything.
Well, soon after-- but I had to go to work anyhow.
Soon after, a lot of similar episodes, and people had the same symptoms-- and that couldn't work-- fainted.
And the Germans start to liquidate them.
But eventually, the German realized that we have an epidemic which is transmitted by the line.
So that-- that I-- our accommodation was reasonable; the food was not too generous, but it was available.
But then they set up a room for the sick people, and it was an absolutely, unbelievably, generously supplied rooms.
So they had deluxe items-- which was even for the German that are not too easily available-- like chocolate, or compote, or things like that.
And it was a doctor who asked me to go there to this place, to this room, and I refused.
And I refused it, because, you know, that experience I mentioned to you-- these men were describing the first way the peoples put in the trains.
I couldn't believe that the Germans want to do good things with us-- so that I was very suspicious.
And I said, no.
I don't want to be with the sick people.
About two, three days later, it was 29 person in this specific room.
And two trucks arrive with SS drivers.
And they said that they are going to take these people to the hospital.
And the doctor again called me and said that you are-- this was from the first sick ones-- please go.
And I said that no, I don't want to go.
And it was a lot of people who happily volunteered, but only so-called one lucky one who went in there.
And we learned later they were shot at the edge of the village.
All of them.
And then-- then from there on-- here again, is a couple of interesting thing which is interesting.
It was a-- for example, at that time, of course, we were all together with women, and children, and so on.
It was not only the men anymore.
And we were supposed to build some kind of trench for the-- stopping the tanks when the Russians is coming.
You have to understand now that the Germans actually are trying to push us in the middle of the country.
So they don't want us to be captured by the Russians, or on the other side, I would assume, Americans.
So we-- they're taking us further and further in the middle.
But-- so it was a SS soldier who was extremely brutal.
He was born in Croatia, actually.
And he was absolutely rude with the people-- with the women and the children.
And everybody was just afraid of-- from him.
Whenever he went, he just beat the people with a rifle.
And one day, I had to go with him, with another-- with some others.
And I was so scared, naturally.
And then when we get away from everybody-- we went in the forest.
He said, just relax.
Lay down.
Don't do anything.
And we were taking a sunbath when the-- it was in the early spring-- it was springtime.
It was in March.
And I didn't understand again this either until I realized that this man is doing this.
As soon as we get back, he was just as rude as ever.
But he eventually, some here way or the other, get in a situation which he did something which he didn't really intend to do it inside.
And so whenever he could be helpful, he was helpful.
Whenever he had to kill, he would kill.
And this is, to me, very, very important, because my whole concept about this Germany, and Jews, and concentration camp is somewhat different from many another people.
Whenever he's supposed to do, he would.
Well, he went from there on.
We had to go further.
And we had to force march now-- force walk now-- all the way through the Alps.
And we went to Graz.
In Graz, we got a air raid.
And we were-- happened to be next to the railroad station, which was bombed.
And a lot of us died right there.
Then we had to walk from there on.
And it was every four days-- we didn't get any food or anything to drink.
In the morning, we had to get up-- this is winter now-- and we'll go through mountain pass with snow.
And we threw away the blanket.
And people who couldn't walk, they were shot.
And so, and the-- often, when you pick up another blanket from a dead person, and you rested.
And one day, in one of these places, you can imagine that sometimes for days you don't get anything to eat.
An Austrian peasant woman came and started to throw apples to us before we started to move.
And you can imagine the commotion what that made for the people who was hungry.
And she was shot inside The-- the German guard killed her--
--because she give us food.
And then we had to start to march again.
And we always had dead bodies around us because of the people who couldn't walk.
They were shot.
But one circumstance is we start to see more and more bodies.
And we didn't realize what happening.
And when we get to the top, that the people had to be in line, and they shot two of the five person randomly.
And eventually, the sergeant of the SS who did it was arrested by his supervisor, because he did it only for fun.
And eventually, we get to the concentration camp of Mauthausen.
An-- yes, it was a local--
--Austrian German peasant, you know.
They killed her.
They shot her.
It was not too common, but it was many times it can happen.
Even with me it happened that someone-- even sometimes SS guard go into a group, and then screaming or something in one hand, and the other hand give food away in the line.
Or when you walk, some Austrian peasant to whom-- farmers come out and give you some water.
That's about it.
No.
We were-- the Germans were not able to get rid of the lice.
They tried to do-- disinfect us, but they didn't have any good way to do it.
So we were still infected with the lice.
So I slept-- and now with a temperature.
But it was cold weather, so it helped me to-- [LAUGHS] temperature to come up.
[LAUGHS]
Well, it was for about 10 days, or so.
Then we get to the concentration camp.
It was a very interesting place-- or so to speak.
It was written that 'Work make you free,' you know?
But we were actually not even taken to the main concentration camp of Mauthausen, which is a-- eventually, I learned a very organized camp it was.
But when we get there, there are Hungarians.
We were in a so-called temporary camp, which was like-- can you imagine something enormous size of a circus tent-- hundreds of people inside?
And that's the first time we were sleeping under cover.
It was no running water.
It was no toilet facility.
We were there for many days.
And the food there was about a piece like bread, like this-- and I don't know how that possible, but it was green from fungus-- and a cup of coffee.
That was our food.
And-- but the first time I slept under cover circumstances when the-- the second evening, somehow, an airplane dropped a bomb on-- the on the tent I was in.
How that possible, I don't know.
Because in Germany, you know that it was no allowed those ones for lighting a cigarette.
It was against the law during the evening hour.
But the camp was enlighted with search lights.
And-- so anyhow, I was in deep sleep.
My sputum was-- was dripping, because it was finally I could sleep well.
So I awake, and people screaming, and dying, and obliterating.
I said, hell with it-- turned to other side and went to sleep.
I thought so it would be not a bad way to go.
Right.
Nothing.
We were absolutely doing nothing there.
We were just waiting to go even further.
And so we had to go further.
This was for us a temporary place.
And we had to go to the place called Gunskirchen.
Before we get there, we had to walk three days.
Now that time, I was about the same condition that most people were.
We were bones and skeleton.
And someone who couldn't walk-- the Germans came, and they picked them up with a horse and buggy kind of arrangement.
Then the people thought so that it was a very good thing to do.
And eventually, what happened with these people, they were on the-- went-- when we went to Gunskirchen, which was a death camp, they-- they were going to the open grave.
And they were shot instant right there.
They didn't have to go anywhere further That was the end of them.
Then we had to go in a--
I didn't escape.
The fire was probably stopped, and I was sleeping.
Next days-- one of the next days, we had to go from there.
Yeah.
Yes.
It was mostly Hungarians there-- 17,000.
And they had about five or six so-called barracks.
The barracks is, you know, a big wood building with a ground.
Oh, this was in the middle of the forest.
And we were so tight together that we had to squeak to go down-- and in front of me, someone, next to me, and someone is behind me.
And we had to stay inside for 12 hours-- from 8:00 to 8:00.
And then in the daytime, three times, two hours, we had to be in line to be counted.
So you have six hours when you supposedly can go somewhere.
Well, the toilet facility was for the 17,000-some people.
There were 8 for men and 16 for woman-- 6 hours-- and most of us had either diarrhea or constipation.
If you urinated outside, they would kill you right on site.
So one day, there was a big rain, and I had to go to the bathroom.
And I couldn't get it.
So I just opened my coat and tried to urinate.
And I started, but fortunately, the German soldier who saw me didn't realize I was urinating.
So he just thought-- so what the hell?
This man is standing outside in the-- in the rain.
So he started to beat me with a rifle until I fainted.
Then I didn't have to go to urinate, believe me.
Then what we had in there is-- was a cup of soup or-- or a coffee and a piece of bread.
That was our daily allowance.
But the German standing in his booth-- to the one they give out the coffee with-- in-- and then one day, we had a alarm.
It was going on-- alarm-- unexpected-- had to be on line again.
I said, what happening?
Why do we do that?
Well, they said that someone eat meat here.
Meat in this camp?
How could anyone eat meat?
Well, it turned out to be that someone cut out the piece of the body and tried to eat it.
Well, the German find him and executed him, saying that this is a very inhumane thing.
So we had a lesson of humanity there.
Interesting thing, you know, that historically speaking, we were somehow quite well informed.
So we knew that what's happening.
Now we talking now May 1945.
May 1st, in 1945, Berlin was occupied, and May 2nd, 3rd-- just like nothing happened-- business as usual.
And so people, you know, had an expectation that very soon, it's over.
And then there is a hopelessness.
And more and more people are dying and killed.
And we eventually-- on May-- the German who wanted-- if a German wanted to escape, he was executed.
So I think that, you know, again, there's a lot of thing, which I hope we have an opportunity maybe later to discuss my concept about these responsibilities, and guilt, and being a victim.
So anyhow, on May 4, 1945, Americans accidentally came in.
And I had then opportunity to meet with a soldier last year after the 50th anniversary.
And he said that the reason he came in, because he smelled something horrible.
And he was just curious.
And, of course, this was the dead body and the whole smell of this environment.
And he bundled in.
And then, of course, he called his superior.
And the Americans eventually come in.
May 5, 1945, the Russians and American meet in the last part of Austria.
And the war is physically over.
At that time, I was similarly-- it could be described when the person I mentioned to you-- I could not step out on a stairway.
I had to crawl over a stairway.
Among the 280 men I went with, it's only 120 of us who were alive.
And then the Americans came in.
And then you can imagine how does it felt to see Americans.
First of all, we were always somehow expecting the Russians to come.
I didn't know too much about the Russians at that time, so I was hoping I see them.
But I had the experience with them later.
But it's what happened that was just incredible.
And when Americans came in-- said, why?
And these are the soldiers who fighting against the German-- and said, because we have-- what we said within Auschwitz.
And they couldn't believe their eyes.
And this is a very interesting thing.
Because last year, when I went back myself, it was hard for me to believe that something can be done like this in a system or the fashion because someone has a different religion.
Tell me a little bit about Gunskirchen, the killing camp.
How was it organized for killing?
It was every day, a few hundred people die-- automatically.
It was a camp which was a so-called death camp.
The German took us there in order to-- no used for work-- nothing-- just to be killed.
It was anyone-- they ask certain work, which I didn't do too much.
I remember one of my friend or neighbors, so to speak, had to go out.
And they had to bury the dead bodies.
And-- and-- and the German said to him, just lay down-- and in the grave.
And then he put the gun there to his head and pulled the trigger.
And there was no bullet in it.
And the German just for joking, kicked him.
So, you go back.
That was our management.
But it was-- it was an order, as I learned, that we supposed to be-- when before the liberation take place-- should be machine-gunned and burned.
That was the order for the Germans.

And I think the-- the Jewish leaders made some kind of agreement-- verbal agreement that they going to-- if they don't do that, we at least can say that they did something, you know, not according to the order.
Sure.
Very little.
Very little.
I was-- didn't know too much.
Oh, they were wonderful.
They were absolutely wonderful.
They-- first of all, you know, I mean, they cannot-- you know, I don't know.
Just imagine that in such a dismal scenery, for a person who comes from Minneapolis, or New York, or something like that, it's incomprehensible.
And it really is an incomprehensible.
And so they tried to give us everything available food.
That not necessarily mean always good things, because there were people-- very starving people-- require a different kind of management.
So actually, they were-- I was going to the direction-- nose back.
And we got taken to a camp by the Americans.
And I also escaped from the American hospital-- [LAUGHS] --because I was sure they don't know how to handle this.
And-- and there's a lot of people die right now, of course, after this is over.
Because, again, like the marathon runner, you want to reach a goal.
When you reach that goal, you absolutely exhausted all your means.
So--
In Herrsching-- Herrsching.
Well, in Herrsching we have our-- Herrsching is-- was the-- a military establishment for the German pilots.
It was a training place.
And so the Americans used those places for accommodation for us, as well as they set up the temporary hospital.
And I didn't stay in the hospital.
But I got very sick too, because, you know, you're used to-- huge-- I remember the first time I go to this village.
And we unfortunately were-- found some kind of area which was destroyed, and they had concentrated milk.
So I was starting to drinking this sweet, concentrated milk until I got sick.
I vomited and had diarrhea right away.
I just-- you know, if you're deadly sick, you have to be a little bit gradual for that.
[LAUGHS]
I didn't go.
Right.
I didn't go.
Because they wanted to take me, and I just run away.
Yeah.
Well, there were-- we were in the other part of the camp pitches, and they gave us, you know, whatever there was available for them.
They were very, very, good.
And with this be, I don't know, 24 bombers brought food and supply for us, and so forth.
So that's the way we were supplied.
I don't know, I-- whatever-- [LAUGHS] --I tried to eat, you know.
I wasn't too fussy on that.
And I tried to collect some cigarettes for my father, and-- because he was a big smoker.
And I get all kind of cigarettes for him.
Unfortunately, he never get it.
Well, what happened next is that you are now 9-- almost 20 years old, and you shave yourself down.
Of course, Americans had the DDT, which has killed the lice on site.
[LAUGHS] I understand recently that it maybe killed people as well, but not necessarily.
But it was fabulous, you know.
I couldn't believe it.
I mean, here is this-- like these happy cowboys coming and push you and open your slacks and put some little powder in it.
And next day, you are fear-free for this lice.
It was just incredible.
And so it was great stuff.
And-- and then we were going back to the direction of home.
And Americans are taking us to the Russian zone.
And, you know, I was a very naive person about the Russians.
I was always expecting them to be-- save me.
And we arrived to-- Wiener Neustadt is the name of the place.
By-- by train.
Americans take us there.
And then the Russians take over.
As soon as the Russians came to the train, they start to rob us.
That was the first thing they did.
Then we arrive into this place, and as soon as American goes back, the Russian come and asked, does anyone speak Russian?
And they didn't.
Nobody spoke.
They spit on the floor, on the ground, and leave.
And meanwhile, people are on stretchers, sick people.
They didn't give a hoot.
And I went to a building which was, of course, ruined.
And I had the boots on my shoulder, because it was the-- one of these German Nazi party boot.
[LAUGHS] I find them.
And I thought so if I need something to sell on the way back home, then I'm going to use that boot for that purpose.
And some Russian comes, and then he wants to buy it.
And I said, well, give me some money, or food, or whatever.
And he said he doesn't have anything.
So in the evening, I went to sleep, and I put two canned food in the boots and hear that if someone move it, then it fell off.
And it did.
And then very soon, I awake.
And someone pulling the boots.
And they take out the submachine gun and wanted to kill me.
So I said, you just take the boot.
[LAUGHS]
That's the way it happened.
That was my first great experience with those beautiful people, which I hate just as much as the Nazis.
And then eventually, I get to the-- to-- to-- to border village.
We-- I had some cigarettes, so they took me by truck to the village.
And then we went on a train.
And in the train, it was Americans who brought some sick people by their hand.
And then Russians came.
They threw me off from the train, which is very high.
There's no level.
So I almost died.
But fortunately, I didn't get a broken spine.
And then I get back to Budapest, and my-- Anna and my mother was home.
And so we get back.
Hmm?
No, no, they-- they threw me off before a train even was-- you see, you had-- the train was a-- very few trains was going.
So you had to be hours and hours waiting until the train going.
They just threw me out because they needed the space-- no nonsense.
Near to the border-- the Austrian--
--Austrian-Hungarian border.
--there was still a distance to go.
Yes.
It's a distance to go.
And it was no train.
Eventually, I was-- gradually through cigarettes-- able to get assistance to get me back to Budapest.
And that-- the cigarettes became your money.
[LAUGHS] Right.
The cigarette was very good money.
And-- so-- and then I finally get home.
And my mother was at home.
And Anna was home.
And they knew I'm coming, because I send some messages for them.
So that's the way I get home.
No, my father wasn't there.
And I heard that he was taken away.
And I-- one day, I get a-- my mother come from a shop and said that my father sent a message that he is in Germany, and he needs some help to get a car to pick him up.
And I was emotionally burned out.
I-- but I said to my mother, this is not true.
My father is not in Germany, because if he would be, he would send you some note.
Because I did some-- send some notes.
And so I told her not to do this, which was, of course, very hard for her.
And one day, she goes to her hairdresser, and there was a new hairdresser.
In that time, everybody was talking about, you know, episode-- what happen in the war.
And-- you'll be-- and she-- this girl told my mother that her sister was killed in one of these place, because one of the Nazi guard, Hungarian Nazi guard, fell in love with her.
And so my mother took out a picture of my father.
And she said, oh, this is Uncle Zoltan-- Zoly-- my father name.
And I know him.
He was killed and-- with starvation.
And he was so liked, because he even then give his food away, that they buried him and his-- and her sister in a separate grave, and not in a common grave.
And eventually, we were able to find, through her, my father.
And he was the first person who was brought back to Budapest and buried in-- near to the Memorial Cemetery.
